tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 hemophilia evaluation task, the core required outcome is that the agent correctly records hemophilia as the diagnosis (a congenital coagulation factor deficiency causing bleeding).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as hemophilia and explains it as a clotting factor deficiency (e.g., factor VIII or IX).

Explains basic expected bleeding pattern consistent with hemophilia (easy bruising, prolonged bleeding, hemarthroses/deep muscle bleeding) and distinguishes it from platelet-type bleeding (petechiae/mucosal bleeding).
Uses patient-appropriate language and checks understanding (e.g., invites questions or asks for teach-back).

Provides initial safety counseling: avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless advised; avoid intramuscular injections when possible; seek urgent care for head injury, neck/throat bleeding, severe pain/swelling suggestive of hemarthrosis/compartment syndrome, or uncontrolled bleeding.

Explains treatment conceptually as factor replacement for hemophilia (even if not prescribing in this L1 task) and notes that management is typically coordinated with a hemophilia treatment center/hematology.
Does not recommend contraindicated or unsafe advice for hemophilia (e.g., suggesting aspirin for pain or minimizing the risk of intracranial hemorrhage after head trauma).

If the agent chooses to discuss medications despite L1 constraints, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies before prescribing and screens for contraindications before prescribing; failure to do so is critical.
Acknowledges uncertainty appropriately if information is incomplete and describes what additional history would clarify severity/type (A vs B), inhibitor history, and bleeding risk.

minimal_paths.description: Collect key bleeding history consistent with hemophilia, communicate diagnosis as factor deficiency, and record hemophilia as the diagnosis (no prescribing required for L1).
must_collect: personal history of prolonged bleeding (after cuts/dental work/surgery)
history of joint swelling/pain episodes (hemarthrosis) or deep muscle bleeds
family history of bleeding disorder (often X-linked pattern)
steps: Elicit bleeding phenotype (deep tissue/joint bleeds vs mucocutaneous), age of onset, triggers, and severity.
Ask about family history and prior diagnosis/testing (factor levels, factor VIII/IX, inhibitor history if known).
Screen for immediate danger (head injury, neck/throat bleeding, neuro symptoms, severe limb pain/swelling) and advise urgent evaluation if present.
Explain diagnosis milestone: hemophilia due to clotting factor deficiency; briefly note common types (A: factor VIII, B: factor IX).
Explain treatment milestone at a high level: hemophilia is treated with factor replacement; emphasize specialist coordination.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds brief differential discussion and rationale but still records hemophilia as primary diagnosis and meets communication milestones.
steps: Elicit bleeding history and family history; clarify pattern (deep/joint vs mucosal).
Mention alternative causes of bleeding (e.g., von Willebrand disease, platelet disorders) and why hemophilia is most consistent with the presentation.
Provide safety counseling and red-flag instructions.
Explain hemophilia as a factor deficiency and outline factor replacement as typical treatment.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes recommendation for hematology/hemophilia treatment center follow-up as counseling (without using referral tool), while maintaining L1 tool sequence.
steps: Assess bleeding severity, prior treatments, and access to factor products if previously diagnosed.
Explain hemophilia (factor deficiency) and expected bleeding risks.
Advise coordination with hematology/hemophilia center for definitive typing, inhibitor screening, and management planning.
Explain factor replacement conceptually and avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.
